article_title: This simple DNS switch is always my first privacy recommendation
core_thesis: Changing your default DNS resolver to a privacy-focused option like Quad9 is the single most impactful first step for improving online privacy and security, because DNS queries expose your browsing destinations even before HTTPS encryption kicks in, and most default resolvers (ISP, Google, Cloudflare) have commercial incentives or data retention policies that undermine privacy.
detailed_summary: The article argues that DNS is an overlooked but critical privacy layer. When you visit a website, your device first performs a DNS lookup to resolve the domain name to an IP address. This query is visible to your DNS resolver (often your ISP or a public resolver like Google 8.8.8.8) and can reveal every domain you visit, even if the page content is encrypted via HTTPS. On public Wi-Fi, unencrypted DNS requests are especially exposed. The author recommends switching to Quad9 (9.9.9.9), a nonprofit DNS resolver based in Switzerland that blocks malware and phishing domains by default, does not log IP addresses, and has no advertising business model. Quad9's privacy policy drops personally identifiable data at the network edge, retaining only city-level aggregate info for capacity planning. The article also highlights Quad9's legal defense of operational neutrality in a 2021 Sony Music case. Setup is simple: change DNS on your router to cover all devices, or per-device via OS settings. Quad9 offers three service tiers: 9.9.9.9 (secure + blocking), 9.9.9.10 (unfiltered), and 9.9.9.11 (secure + ECS for CDN optimization). Compared to Cloudflare and Google DNS, Quad9 is the only nonprofit with no commercial incentive to monetize DNS data, and it blocks threats by default. The article notes potential speed trade-offs (some users report slower loads vs. Cloudflare) and router compatibility issues with DNS-over-HTTPS.

entities.name: Quad9
entities.role: Nonprofit DNS resolver focused on privacy and security, recommended as the primary alternative to default DNS.

entities.name: Cloudflare 1.1.1.1
entities.role: Commercial CDN company offering a fast, privacy-respecting DNS resolver with 24-hour log retention.

entities.name: Google Public DNS 8.8.8.8
entities.role: Commercial advertising company's DNS resolver, with anonymized logs after two weeks.

entities.name: Afam Onyimadu
entities.role: Author of the article, tech journalist and privacy advocate.

entities.name: Sony Music
entities.role: Entity that attempted to compel Quad9 to globally block domains via a Hamburg court injunction in 2021.

key_data_points: DNS queries expose the domain name of every website you visit, even if the page content is encrypted via HTTPS, because the lookup happens before the connection is established. 🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: understanding DNS leakage helps in reconnaissance and exfiltration scenarios.

Quad9 does not log IP addresses for DNS queries; personally identifiable data is dropped at the network edge, retaining only city-level aggregate geographic info for capacity planning. 🟢 Relevant to privacy: ensures no persistent record of your browsing destinations.

Quad9 blocks malware, phishing, and botnet domains by default using threat intelligence from security partners, preventing connections to flagged domains. 🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: this can hinder C2 communication if the domain is known; consider using domain fronting or less-known TLDs.

In 2021, Quad9 fought and won a Hamburg court case against Sony Music, which sought a global domain-blocking injunction, demonstrating Quad9's commitment to operational neutrality. 🟢 Relevant to legal/privacy context: shows Quad9 is willing to defend user privacy against corporate overreach.

Quad9 offers three service options: 9.9.9.9 (secure + malware blocking), 9.9.9.10 (unfiltered), and 9.9.9.11 (secure + ECS for CDN optimization). 🟢 Relevant to homelab: choose the appropriate tier based on need for blocking vs. performance.
Some routers only support HTTP/1.1 and may fail with DNS-over-HTTPS; switching to DNS-over-TLS can resolve the issue. 🟢 Relevant to homelab: troubleshoot by testing with a leak test tool.

Cloudflare 1.1.1.1 deletes logs within 24 hours (KPMG-audited), while Google anonymizes after two weeks; Quad9 never stores IP logs. 🟢 Relevant to privacy: Quad9 offers the strongest no-logging policy.

Quad9 is a nonprofit foundation based in Switzerland, unlike Cloudflare (US commercial) and Google (US commercial advertising company). 🟢 Relevant to trust: Swiss jurisdiction offers strong privacy protections.
methodology_steps: Step 1: Identify your current DNS resolver by visiting a DNS leak test website (e.g., dnsleaktest.com) to see which servers your device is using.
Step 2: Choose the appropriate Quad9 DNS address: 9.9.9.9 (secure + malware blocking) as the primary, and 149.112.112.112 as secondary.

Step 3: Configure DNS at the router level for network-wide coverage: log into your router's admin panel, find the DNS settings (usually under WAN or Internet settings), and replace the default DNS servers with Quad9's addresses.

Step 4: If router-level change is not possible (e.g., ISP locks DNS), configure per-device: on Windows go to Network Settings > Change adapter options > IPv4 properties > Use custom DNS; on macOS go to System Preferences > Network > Advanced > DNS; on Android use Private DNS mode (set hostname as dns.quad9.net); on iOS go to Wi-Fi settings > Configure DNS > Manual.
Step 5: If you experience slow loading on streaming or CDN-heavy sites, consider using 9.9.9.11 (with ECS enabled) instead of 9.9.9.9.
Step 6: If your router fails to connect using DNS-over-HTTPS (common with HTTP/1.1-only routers), switch to DNS-over-TLS in the router settings.
Step 7: Verify the change by running a DNS leak test and confirming that the responding servers are Quad9 IP addresses (9.9.9.9, 149.112.112.112, etc.).
conclusions: Switching to Quad9 is a simple, high-impact privacy improvement that reduces exposure of browsing habits to ISPs and default DNS providers, and blocks many phishing/malware domains automatically. The article supports this with Quad9's no-logging policy, nonprofit structure, and legal defense of neutrality.

Quad9 is not a full VPN or anonymization tool; it only secures DNS queries. For complete traffic encryption, additional measures like a VPN or Tor are needed. However, as a first step, it significantly reduces metadata leakage.

Users may experience slightly slower DNS resolution compared to Cloudflare, but the privacy and security benefits outweigh the speed trade-off for most. The article acknowledges this via reader comments and the comparison table.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your cybersecurity and homelab interests. As a Red Teamer, understanding DNS leakage is crucial for reconnaissance and exfiltration; Quad9's blocking can interfere with C2 domains, so you should test your tools against it. For your homelab, implementing Quad9 network-wide is a quick win for privacy and security, and the article provides clear configuration steps for routers and devices. As a student, this is a low-effort privacy upgrade that protects your browsing data from ISPs and public Wi-Fi snooping. The comparison with Cloudflare and Google helps you make an informed choice based on your threat model.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a local DNS sinkhole (e.g., Pi-hole) in your homelab for even finer-grained control over DNS blocking and logging. You can use Quad9 as the upstream resolver for Pi-hole to combine the benefits of both. For Red Teaming, test how Quad9's threat intelligence affects your payload delivery and C2 channels—if it blocks your domains, you may need to use domain fronting, less-known TLDs, or DNS-over-HTTPS with a different resolver during operations.
